title: From Control To Surrender
study: A Study In The Life Of King David
scriptureForWeek: 1 SAMUEL 17 This whole chapter deals with David’s victory over Goliath. This is a key moment in David’s life. After this, there is no turning back for him.
weekTheme: Boys cower, Men fight God will help us fight One of the hardest things to face growing up is dealing with bullies. Not the silly kind you see in movies, but guys who genuinely intimidate and scare you. If you haven’t dealt with a bully yet, you will. Bullies come in all shapes and sizes, but they specialize in one thing: Intimidation. Bullies seek to make you comply with their will. They do this by verbal threats, physical attacks, or mocking you in front of others. You can get bullied in person or bullied online. Being bullied sucks. It can cause a lot of anxiety and make you want to shrink back in shame or hide in fear. It can even make you throw others under the bus so that you get out of being picked on yourself. (By the way, you should never bully others. In the biggest study ever conducted on “things people regret,” bullying others was right at the top! It's bad being bullied, but it’s just as bad having the regret of bullying others.) This week, David is going to face Goliath. You have probably heard this story at some point; it’s one of the most famous fights in the whole bible, and you may be tempted to tune out thinking you know it all. But you have to remember that David wasn’t much older than you when he went against Goliath. Imagine that! If David was in your class, you would have been in awe of his courage and boldness. So what made a guy your age willing to fight a massive grown man when everyone else was afraid? And why didn’t he fight like the other soldiers with Saul’s armor? This week, you are going to learn the source of David’s courage and how God will empower you to handle the people that seek to beat you down and intimidate you. This week is a week that will have implications for the rest of your life. It’s a window into how to be fearless and full of courage. How to push back on intimidation and live with strength. Pay attention. You are going to need this to be a strong man in the world.

days.scripture: 1 SAMUEL 17:1-11 The Philistines now mustered their army for battle and camped between Socoh in Judah and Azekah at Ephes- dammim. Saul countered by gathering his Israelite troops near the valley of Elah. So the Philistines and Israelites faced each other on opposite hills, with the valley between them. Then Goliath, a Philistine champion from Gath, came out of the Philistine ranks to face the forces of Israel. He was over nine feet tall! He wore a bronze helmet, and his bronze coat of mail weighed 125 pounds. He also wore bronze leg armor, and he carried a bronze javelin on his shoulder. The shaft of his spear was as heavy and thick as a weaver’s beam, tipped with an iron spearhead that weighed 15 pounds. His armor bearer walked ahead of him carrying a shield. Goliath stood and shouted a taunt across to the Israelites. “Why are you all coming out to fight?” he called. “I am the Philistine champion, but you are only the servants of Saul. Choose one man to come down here and fight me! If he kills me, then we will be your slaves. But if I kill him, you will be our slaves! I defy the armies of Israel today! Send me a man who will fight me!” When Saul and the Israelites heard this, they were terrified and deeply shaken.
days.reflection: How do you handle fear? Fear can be small, like a scary moment in a movie that you didn’t expect, or it can be huge, like a bully picking on you at school when none of your friends are around. Fear is designed to protect us from legitimate emotional and physical threats so we can get through life and enjoy it. You are growing up at a time when people deal with a lot of fear, anxiety, and stress. Nearly one in four people experience a form of anxiety disorder during their lives, and nearly 8 percent experience post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD). Anxiety is really high among teenagers right now; it’s even at historic levels. Fear comes from a part of your brain called the amygdala (weird sounding word ☺), and it’s a part of the brain that kind of looks like an almond. But it's responsible for telling us when we are experiencing a threat. It makes our breathing intensify, our heart rate jump, and it pumps our muscles with blood so we are ready to react. People tend to have 4 responses when they get afraid: Fight. Defend ourselves against the threat. Flight. Run away to a safer place. Freeze. Do nothing and hope the threat goes away. Fawn. Try and please whatever is threatening us in hopes that it doesn’t get worse. The Israelites were in a battle with their arch-enemies, the Philistines, and they were afraid. Two armies were facing each other on opposite sides of a valley. They were yelling threats at each other, stuck in a deadlock. One of the Philistine soldiers came out to taunt them, and you know who it is - Goliath. Look at the response, Goliath stood and shouted a taunt across to the Israelites. “Why are you all coming out to fight?” he called. “I am the Philistine champion, but you are only the servants of Saul. Choose one man to come down here and fight me! If he kills me, then we will be your slaves. But if I kill him, you will be our slaves! I defy the armies of Israel today! Send me a man who will fight me!” When Saul and the Israelites heard this, they were terrified and deeply shaken. The Israelites’ response was to freeze. They were terrified and deeply shaken. This is a winner take all battle. They don’t think they have a chance against Goliath, and they don’t want to be sold as slaves. And for good reason - Goliath is HUGE. He isn’t a bean pole either; he was jacked. Look at how heavy his armor was! The typical person at that time of history was just over 5 feet tall. Goliath is nearly twice that height. For perspective, the tallest men to play in the NBA were Gheorghe Mureșan, who was 7 feet 7 inches tall, and Manute Bol, also 7’7. If you want some perspective, go get a tape measure and measure yourself, and then mark the NBA guys’ height and then Goliath’s. Fear can spread amongst a crowd or community of people. Fear is contagious. This is the scene David is going to step into. It’s going to be a defining moment in his life. The whole army is scared, frozen in fear, and terrified. He is stepping into the midst of a demoralized and discouraged army. You are going to deal with fear as you grow up. The threats you face are going to get more and more challenging, and you are going to have to learn to act in spite of your fear. You may not face a 9-foot man threatening to enslave you, but your challenges can be just as intimidating and real. You are going to have to find a source of strength to help you overcome your fears and become the man God has called you to be. The good news is, God will help you. This week you will learn how that happens.
questions: Of the 4 responses to fear (fight, flight, freeze, fawn), which one do you normally respond with?

Is there a time you have been really afraid of something? Maybe even right now? Would you be willing to share what it is, and why? It doesn’t have to be something others are afraid of, just something that’s real for you.
Ask the person you are doing this study with to share what they were afraid of when they were your age. How did they learn to overcome it?

thoughts.verse: Don’t be afraid, for I am with you. Don’t be discouraged, for I am your God. I will strengthen you and help you. I will hold you up with my victorious right hand.
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you in it?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to surrender anything you are currently afraid of to God. Just bring it before him, and pray for the strength he promises from the passage you just read.

days.title: Facing Your Fear
days.scripture: 1 SAMUEL 17:12-29 Now David was the son of an Ephrathite named Jesse, who was from Bethlehem in Judah. Jesse had eight sons, and in Saul’s time he was very old. Jesse’s three oldest sons had followed Saul to the war: The firstborn was Eliab; the second, Abinadab; and the third, Shammah. David was the youngest. The three oldest followed Saul, but David went back and forth from Saul to tend his father’s sheep at Bethlehem. For forty days the Philistine came forward every morning and evening and took his stand. Now Jesse said to his son David, “Take this ephah of roasted grain and these ten loaves of bread for your brothers and hurry to their camp. Take along these ten cheeses to the commander of their unit. See how your brothers are and bring back some assurance from them. They are with Saul and all the men of Israel in the Valley of Elah, fighting against the Philistines.” Early in the morning David left the flock in the care of a shepherd, loaded up and set out, as Jesse had directed. He reached the camp as the army was going out to its battle positions, shouting the war cry. Israel and the Philistines were drawing up their lines facing each other. David left his things with the keeper of supplies, ran to the battle lines and asked his brothers how they were. As he was talking with them, Goliath, the Philistine champion from Gath, stepped out from his lines and shouted his usual defiance, and David heard it. Whenever the Israelites saw the man, they all fled from him in great fear. Now the Israelites had been saying, “Do you see how this man keeps coming out? He comes out to defy Israel. The king will give great wealth to the man who kills him. He will also give him his daughter in marriage and will exempt his family from taxes in Israel.” David asked the men standing near him, “What will be done for the man who kills this Philistine and removes this disgrace from Israel? Who is this uncircumcised Philistine that he should defy the armies of the living God?” They repeated to him what they had been saying and told him, “This is what will be done for the man who kills him.” When Eliab, David’s oldest brother, heard him speaking with the men, he burned with anger at him and asked, “Why have you come down here? And with whom did you leave those few sheep in the wilderness? I know how conceited you are and how wicked your heart is; you came down only to watch the battle.” “Now what have I done?” said David. “Can’t I even speak?” He then turned away to someone else and brought up the same matter, and the men answered him as before. What David said was overheard and reported to Saul, and Saul sent for him.
days.reflection: Yesterday you looked at the 4 responses people typically have toward fear: fight, flight, freeze, or fawn. But there is actually a 5th response. One that has the ability to rob fear of its power and change the whole situation. And that response is this: Facing your fear. To deal with fear, you need to look it right in the eye and ask what is actually happening in you with as clear a mind as possible. You need to do this because fear often distorts things out of perspective. Fear makes us focus on the wrong things so that we lose sight of the right ones. And that is this: God will always be with you when you are afraid. This is exactly what David does when he rolls up to the battlefield with his brothers. Everyone else is terrified by Goliath, but David isn’t. Why? He asks two questions that were different than everyone there. And these questions are designed to face the fear, remove its power, and overcome it. The first question is, “What will be done for the man who kills this Philistine and removes this disgrace from Israel?” David wants to know what the reward will be for killing Goliath. When everyone is looking at the risk, David looks at the reward. He knows something that few guys your age understand. There are always rewards and respect for a young guy who can face his fear. David saw Goliath as a giant opportunity when everyone else saw him as a giant threat. The second question he asked is, “Who is this uncircumcised Philistine that he should defy the armies of the living God?” David knew that the enemies’ greatest goal is to make God seem small and the fear look big. He wants us to see ourselves as tiny and the problem as huge. But David saw clearly. He knew the stories of Joseph and of Moses and the Prophets. He knew what God has done in the past to rescue his people and stop their enemies. Everyone else has lost sight of this. And by asking two simple questions, David, the shepherd, stands out in the midst of an army of soldiers. David’s fearless attitude got the attention of King Saul, as we read, “Saul sent for him.” First, Samuel sent for David to anoint him. Now Saul sends for David to fight for him. When you are confronted with something you are afraid of, surrender your fear to God. Look it straight in the eye, ask what the reward is, and remember who your God is. Being brave will set you apart like nothing else.
questions: Think back to something you were afraid of when you were younger, but aren’t afraid of anymore. What was it, how did you face your fear and learn to overcome it?
Who is one of the most fearless people you know? How do they show this in their life? Try and be as specific as possible.
Ask the person you are doing this study with to share one of the best rewards they have gotten in their lives for facing their fears.

thoughts.verse: I prayed to the LORD, and he answered me. He freed me from all my fears. Those who look to him for help will be radiant with joy; no shadow of shame will darken their faces. Psalms 34:4-5 Read through his verse slowly a second time. Is there something that stands out to you in it? Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean? Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to ask God to give you grace to face your current fears. Ask him to show you the rewards you will get for overcoming your fear.

days.title: Growing In Faith
days.scripture: 1 SAMUEL 17:32-39 David said to Saul, “Let no one lose heart on account of this Philistine; your servant will go and fight him.” Saul replied, “You are not able to go out against this Philistine and fight him; you are only a young man, and he has been a warrior from his youth.” But David said to Saul, “Your servant has been keeping his father’s sheep. When a lion or a bear came and carried off a sheep from the flock, I went after it, struck it and rescued the sheep from its mouth. When it turned on me, I seized it by its hair, struck it and killed it. Your servant has killed both the lion and the bear; this uncircumcised Philistine will be like one of them, because he has defied the armies of the living God. The LORD who rescued me from the paw of the lion and the paw of the bear will rescue me from the hand of this Philistine.” Saul said to David, “Go, and the LORD be with you.” Then Saul dressed David in his own tunic. He put a coat of armor on him and a bronze helmet on his head. David fastened on his sword over the tunic and tried walking around, because he was not used to them. “I cannot go in these,” he said to Saul, “because I am not used to them.” So he took them off.
days.reflection: Facing your fears is hard. But committing to face your fears and surrender them to God at your age will give you such a head start as you move into the world as a man. If you face your fears now, then you will be able to move forward and handle larger challenges and responsibilities that will give you even greater rewards. But if you let your fears control you now, they will haunt you as you grow and shut down cool things that you can’t even imagine right now. Learning to surrender and trust God is one of the most important things you can do as you grow into a man. King David was able to build his confidence and face his fears a little bit by a little bit. When he was a shepherd, his greatest threat was facing lions and bears trying to steal his sheep. But he showed courage and was able to kill the lion and bear with his sling. This was probably terrifying at first, but bit by bit, he gained confidence in his own skills and confidence in God. He got lots of target practice with his sling and had courage against the animals. And it was his confidence in overcoming these smaller challenges that gave him the capacity to face larger ones. “David said to Saul, “Let no one lose heart on account of this Philistine; your servant will go and fight him…. The LORD who rescued me from the paw of the lion and the paw of the bear will rescue me from the hand of this Philistine.” King Saul didn’t understand the source of David’s courage. He wanted him to wear his armor and fight like everyone else. But David didn’t want to rely on what the other soldiers used; he wanted to rely on what God had taught him. You probably have things coming at you now that feel really heavy. Maybe you have to give a presentation in a class, confront someone for being mean or lying about you, or try out for a new sports team. These things aren’t the biggest things in the whole world, but they can still be really scary. Learn to surrender your fears to God and face them head-on with his help. The momentum of courage will grow over time, and you will find that you will have a history of help that lets you trust God for the next thing you face. And you don’t have to face problems the way other guys do. Rely on what you are learning from God, your parents, your church, and here in the Primal Path, and you will be amazed at how those lessons are the exact things you will need in the moment. Learn to handle the smaller things, and at the right time, you will be ready for the big ones.
questions: Can you think of a movie you love where the main character had to be faithful in the smaller lessons before he could handle larger ones? How do you think that could apply to you learning to handle fear?
What do you think are the top 3 or 4 fears guys your age face? How do you see that show up?
Ask the person you are doing this study with to list the differences they see in what your generation fears versus their generation. Do you find any of these things surprising?

thoughts.verse: But when I am afraid, I will put my trust in you. I praise God for what he has promised. I trust in God, so why should I be afraid? What can mere mortals do to me? Psalms 56:3-4 Read through his verse slowly a second time. Is there something that stands out to you in it? Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean? Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to ask God to fill you with faith to face any challenges you come across today.

days.title: The Showdown
days.scripture: 1 SAMUEL 17:40-51 Then he took his staff in his hand, chose five smooth stones from the stream, put them in the pouch of his shepherd’s bag and, with his sling in his hand, approached the Philistine. Meanwhile, the Philistine, with his shield bearer in front of him, kept coming closer to David. He looked David over and saw that he was little more than a boy, glowing with health and handsome, and he despised him. He said to David, “Am I a dog, that you come at me with sticks?” And the Philistine cursed David by his gods. “Come here,” he said, “and I’ll give your flesh to the birds and the wild animals! ” David said to the Philistine, “You come against me with sword and spear and javelin, but I come against you in the name of the LORD Almighty, the God of the armies of Israel, whom you have defied. This day the LORD will deliver you into my hands, and I’ll strike you down and cut off your head. This very day I will give the carcasses of the Philistine army to the birds and the wild animals, and the whole world will know that there is a God in Israel. All those gathered here will know that it is not by sword or spear that the LORD saves; for the battle is the LORD’s, and he will give all of you into our hands.” As the Philistine moved closer to attack him, David ran quickly toward the battle line to meet him. Reaching into his bag and taking out a stone, he slung it and struck the Philistine on the forehead. The stone sank into his forehead, and he fell face down on the ground. So David triumphed over the Philistine with a sling and a stone; without a sword in his hand he struck down the Philistine and killed him. David ran and stood over him. He took hold of the Philistine’s sword and drew it from the sheath. After he killed him, he cut off his head with the sword.
days.reflection: The way a man learns to deal with fear isn’t by hyping himself up and trying to steady his nerves. That is a very short-term solution. The way a Christian learns to do this is by depending on God to help him. And you will need God's help to confront the challenges you are going to face in your life, because confronting your fear and your enemies can be incredibly intimidating. It was one thing for David to say he would fight Goliath, and another thing to actually do it. Goliath was so huge that he was able to carry over 200 pounds of armor on him. Imagine trying to stick a sword through armor that thick. And on top of that, he had someone in front of him holding up a huge shield the size of a typical person to block anything thrown at him. Goliath was also arrogant and defiant. He defied the God of Israel mocking him openly, and he called David a boy, not even worthy of fighting him. I don’t know about you, but I would have been terrified. I certainly wouldn’t have yelled back with any sort of confidence of my own. But David was humble and dependent. He knew that to face his fear and beat Goliath, God was going to have to help him. David’s defiance of Goliath wasn’t based on his arrogance; it was based on dependence. So in response to Goliath's taunts, David drops one of the best speeches ever given by a teenager in all of history, “David said to the Philistine, “You come against me with sword and spear and javelin, but I come against you in the name of the LORD Almighty, the God of the armies of Israel, whom you have defied. This day the LORD will deliver you into my hands, and I’ll strike you down and cut off your head. This very day I will give the carcasses of the Philistine army to the birds and the wild animals, and the whole world will know that there is a God in Israel. All those gathered here will know that it is not by sword or spear that the LORD saves; for the battle is the LORD’s, and he will give all of you into our hands.” David picked 5 stones, got his sling, and ran into battle. Now it may seem kind of weak to bring a sling against a man like Goliath, but these slings were powerful weapons. They could throw rocks about 100 miles an hour, and the stones David used were about the size of a golf ball or tennis ball. Imagine one of those hitting you in the forehead at 100 miles an hour! Goliath wasn’t expecting this. He never saw it coming. The stone sunk into his forehead and he was out! What comes next is kind of amazing. So David triumphed over the Philistine with a sling and a stone; without a sword in his hand he struck down the Philistine and killed him. David ran and stood over him. He took hold of the Philistine’s sword and drew it from the sheath. After he killed him, he cut off his head with the sword. It turns out that the Israelite army was only thinking about the skills and strength they had available to them in the natural realm. But David relied on the strength God gave him and the skills he was prepared with. Everyone was still looking at the outward appearance, but God was looking at the heart. The world saw a small boy without armor and without a chance. God saw a man who loved him, cared about his glory, relied on him, and was ready for a moment like this with perfect preparation. As it turns out, David was actually the giant, and Goliath was the boy. Don’t worry about how you appear to others. Focus on growing in your relationship with God. Don’t face your fears in your own strength; face them based on who God is. The most important thing at this point in your life is to surrender yourself to God. If you do this, everything else will fall into place.
questions: What do you think it means to rely on God when you are afraid? It can be a tricky concept that sounds good on paper but is really vague when you are freaking out. Take a moment and list 3 practical steps on what you can do when you get overwhelmed with fear.

In what way was David’s preparation as a shepherd the perfect preparation for facing Goliath? What implications do you think this may have for what you are learning now? Here is a 2-minute account to give you some size perception of the battle: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=swY3NVcl7Qo Here is an example of how long it takes to learn to throw a sling and how David’s shepherding season was actually perfect preparation: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=28r-MQejnHg Here is an example of what these things can look like at full speed: (Imagine Goliaths Head) -- https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=vAul1_ajCG0

thoughts.verse: Read through these verses slowly. Say to those with fearful hearts, “Be strong, and do not fear, for your God is coming to destroy your enemies. He is coming to save you.”
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you in any of them?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to humble yourself before God and tell him you depend on him. Ask him to show you his love and fill you with confidence today.

days.title: Relying On Jesus
days.scripture: 1 SAMUEL 17:51-58 When the Philistines saw that their hero was dead, they turned and ran. Then the men of Israel and Judah surged forward with a shout and pursued the Philistines to the entrance of Gath and to the gates of Ekron. Their dead were strewn along the Shaaraim road to Gath and Ekron. When the Israelites returned from chasing the Philistines, they plundered their camp. David took the Philistine’s head and brought it to Jerusalem; he put the Philistine’s weapons in his own tent. As Saul watched David going out to meet the Philistine, he said to Abner, commander of the army, “Abner, whose son is that young man?” Abner replied, “As surely as you live, Your Majesty, I don’t know.” The king said, “Find out whose son this young man is.” As soon as David returned from killing the Philistine, Abner took him and brought him before Saul, with David still holding the Philistine’s head. “Whose son are you, young man?” Saul asked him. David said, “I am the son of your servant Jesse of Bethlehem.”
days.reflection: Fear is contagious. It can spread through a group of men like a plague. But guess what? Faith is contagious. It can spread through a group of men like a fire. This is what happened when David surrendered himself to God. He faced his fear, he relied on his preparation, and he acted with boldness. God rewarded him with a victory that inspired the hearts of all of God's people. Instead of shrinking back as an army, they surged forward and pursued the Philistines who fled in fear. This is an incredible example of what can happen when a young man walks with God. David had no official authority and wasn’t even in the army, but he is the one God used to bring deliverance. Saul was head and shoulders above all the men. He was the proper man with the proper size to fight Goliath, but he was crippled by fear and couldn’t fight. Saul had a sword, but it was useless because he wasn’t surrendered to God. David was able to do with God what Saul couldn’t do with armor and an army. But there is even better news than what you have learned in the story of David and Goliath. You follow Jesus, and he is better than David. Jesus stepped up not against Goliath but against the greatest giants you will face as a man. Jesus defeated sin, Satan, death, and hell, the true enemies of our souls. And his victory is the source of our victory. Jesus did for you what you could never do for yourself. You no longer need to hide in fear or shame from anything or anyone. You can now surge forward in faith and celebrate what he has accomplished for you. All guys your age have to learn to deal with their fear. All guys your age have to learn to overcome fear or your life will never be all it can be. But you don’t have to do this on your own. You don’t have to rely on your own strength. You have the Bible which shows you all the ways God has come through for guys like you in the past. You have your community who love you and will support you, and you have Jesus, who promises to never leave you or forsake you. All men wrestle with fear. It’s just a part of life. But not all men surrender their fear to God and learn to rely on him. This week, you have seen what happens when a teenager like you trusts in God. Give up trying to control your fear and surrender it to God. Why not make a commitment to be like David? Why not commit to follow Jesus and rely on him? God may surprise you with what comes next.
questions: What did you find most interesting or inspiring about this week's session?
Can you think of a couple of ways Jesus is like David, but better than David?
In what ways have you learned how Christian guys your age can learn to handle fear as opposed to guys who don’t know God?

thoughts.verse: Read through these verses slowly. The LORD is my light and my salvation—so why should I be afraid? The LORD is my fortress, protecting me from danger, so why should I tremble? When evil people come to devour me, when my enemies and foes attack me, they will stumble and fall. Though a mighty army surrounds me, my heart will not be afraid. Even if I am attacked, I will remain confident.
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you in any of them?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to thank God for all Jesus has done and accomplished for you. Thank him that you are free from shame, fear, and guilt because of what he did on the cross.

extras: WEEK 3 MANSCHOOL FEAR FACTOR MAN SCHOOL REMINDER The recipe for fun is pretty simple when you’re raising boys: add to any activity an element of danger, stir in a little exploration, add a dash of destruction, and you’ve got yourself a winner. - John Eldredge OPTION 1. ACTIVITY: FEAR FACTOR Watch an old episode of Fear Factor on Youtube. Then spend some time talking about your son's 4 or 5 biggest fears. Then come up with a plan to confront a few of them with him. Let him know that fearing fear is worse than the actual experience. Help him understand how fear works, and that God will help him overcome it. OPTION 2. WATCH WONDER A cool and moving story about a 5th-grade kid who has to overcome fear and confront the drama of school dynamics: https://youtu.be/Ob7fPOzbmzE Or watch this amazing 15-minute TED talk by Malcolm Gladwell on David and Goliath: https://youtu.be/ziGD7vQOwl8 OPTION 3. DINNER AND DISCUSSION Take your son out to one of his favorite restaurants or for desserts and talk through what it means to wrestle with fear. It could be that your son has someone bullying him right now and he is too ashamed to talk about it. It may be that your son is bullying someone right now and feels shame or regret doing it. This is an incredible opportunity to go deep and talk about stuff that fathers and sons rarely connect on.
FIELD NOTES PRAYER FOR MY SON
